text: Hi, I am Dr. Shinjini and today we will discuss an interesting case of post-hysterectomy endometriotic adnexal mass.
This surgery involved extensive adhesiolysis, ureterolysis and finally adnexectomy.
So our patient was a forty-six year old lady who presented to us with chief complaints of severe pain abdomen.
On ultrasound, she was detected with a large, ten-into-ten-into-nine-centimetres left adnexal cystic lesion with internal echoes which were suggestive of endometriosis.
The surgical field shows the absence of a separately identifiable right ovary, indicating possible adhesion or anatomical alteration.
Her CA-one-twenty-five marker was about fifty-five units per ml and her ROMA index was normal.
She was a para six living six, all normal deliveries and had undergone an abdominal hysterectomy about one year back at a small place in northern India of which no records were available.
The patient has a vertical midline infraumbilical incision visible on the abdomen.
In the laparoscopic view, the surgeon begins the procedure by performing adhesiolysis to mobilize the sigmoid colon, which is completely adherent to the vault region, away from the adnexa.

The surgeon uses a laparoscopic shearer for adhesiolysis, following the white peritoneal reflection line on the lateral pelvic wall to release the sigmoid colon. The dissection progresses cranially along the virgin plane.

The surgeon moves down towards the adnexal side, exposing the genitofemoral nerve and the psoas muscle on the left side. The surgical team then proceeds to release the adnexal adhesions to the rectosigmoid.
The surgeon uses graspers to make small, precise movements along the fat line, carefully releasing the sigmoid colon from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the mass, attempting to retain its integrity during adhesiolysis to prevent rupture, while continuing to separate adhesions around the mass.

The surgeon uses laparoscopic instruments to carefully release adhesions along the white line, which is the peritoneal reflection on the lateral pelvic wall, separating the sigmoid colon from surrounding structures.
The surgeon applies blunt traction and counter traction to gently release the rectosigmoid colon from the firmly adherent adnexa, using careful manipulation to separate the structures.
The surgeon performs small opening movements with the graspers, following the fat planes to carefully separate the rectosigmoid from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon makes a few releasing incisions using an energy device to efficiently mobilize the adnexal mass, with precise movements to separate it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon identifies the infundibulopelvic ligament as the primary blood supply to the mass and prepares to divide it, ensuring careful handling to maintain control over the blood supply.
The surgeon releases the sigmoid colon to gain access to the left pararectal space, ensuring the ureter is identified and safeguarded during the procedure.

The surgeon ligates and divides the ovarian vessels within the infundibulopelvic ligament. Attention is also given to the advanced bladder and its adhesions to the vaginal vault, highlighting areas of concern.
The surgeon opens the pelvic triangle by dividing the round ligament, taking care to position it laterally within the surgical field.
The pelvic wall structures are clearly visible, allowing for precise identification and manipulation during the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses a right-hand grasper to hold the infundibulopelvic vessels, which are visible. The infundibulopelvic ligament is skeletonized and then suture tied.
Following the ligation of the infundibulopelvic ligament, the surgeon further dissects and opens the pelvic triangle, sequentially exposing the anatomical structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the left external iliac vein, with the obliterated umbilical artery visible below and deeper, as it is being gently tugged.
The surgeon performs ureterolysis, carefully dissecting the ureter from the lateral aspect of the mass, highlighting its close proximity to the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon locates the uterine artery and prepares to divide it, focusing on the anatomical positioning and separation of the artery from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon locates the uterine artery at its origin by back tracing the obliterated umbilical artery. The uterine artery is then sealed and divided. Subsequently, the infundibulopelvic ligament is sealed above the ligated area to ensure a secure stump on the patient's side.
The surgeon gently opens the fascia below the infundibulopelvic ligament to locate the ureter, revealing its position relative to surrounding structures.
The surgeon locates the ureter, which courses along the lateral margin of the adnexal mass, highlighting its proximity to the mass in a post-hysterectomy context.
The surgeon locates the ureter and proceeds to trace the obliterated umbilical artery backward, ensuring careful navigation around the anatomical structures.
In a post hysterectomy mass which is of endometriotic origin it is very common to find the ureter involved with a periureteric endometriotic sheath.

The surgeon gently traces the ureter, dissecting the surrounding tissues. Attention is then shifted to the central part of the vault, where an attempt is made to lyse bladder adhesions and identify a demarcating plane. The area is challenging due to dense fibrous adhesions.
The surgeon applies counter traction to the mass using an instrument from the assisting port, focusing on the fat plane surrounding the mass.
The surgeon identifies the cleavage plane between the bladder and the adnexal mass and proceeds to further develop this plane, separating the two structures.
At this point of time we realize that the ureter is

The surgeon ruptures the endometriotic cyst, releasing chocolate-colored fluid, which facilitates handling of the adnexal mass. The peritoneum is excised, and the ovary is mobilized amidst intense induration and adhesions surrounding the mass.

The surgeon has successfully mobilized the adnexal mass from surrounding tissues, facilitated by the prior sacrifice of the uterine artery. Attention is now shifted to the pararectal space, where the pararectal fat planes are mobilized, and the inferior portion of the mass is extracted.
The surgeon begins mobilizing the right adnexa and adhesions of the sigmoid colon to delineate the rectal axis, carefully separating these structures from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon follows the fat plane to carefully separate the ovary from the surrounding adhesions, ensuring clear delineation of the anatomical structures.
The surgeon attempts to free the central vault from surrounding adhesions, carefully separating the tissue to improve access and visibility in the surgical field.
A rectal probe is placed in the rectum, visibly delineating the rectal axis below the surgical field.

The surgeon releases adhesions from the superior aspect of the sigmoid colon, eliminating its buckling. The rectum is visibly stretched on the right side. Following this, the rectal margins are delineated, and the rectovaginal space is developed at the level of the central vault.

The surgeon identifies a clear tissue plane and follows it to mobilize the area, freeing the mass from the inferior and lateral aspects. The remaining connections of the mass are near the bladder area, with visible tension on the mass.

The surgeon encounters severe adhesions and intense fibrosis around the ureter. To create more space for ureterolysis, a major portion of the adnexa is debulked while keeping some part of the mass attached to the ureter.

The surgeon follows the fat plane to safeguard the bladder while removing the main portion of the mass. The ureter is visibly involved in the dense periureteric endometriotic sheath. Adhesiolysis begins, with the uterine artery already cut and lifted by the surgeon's left hand grasper.
The surgeon observes the kinking and external compression of the ureter caused by the surrounding endometriotic tissue. The harmonic scalpel is used to address these adhesions.

The surgeon meticulously excises the endometriotic fibrous tissue, ensuring the bladder is safeguarded throughout the procedure. The ureter is consistently kept in view during the excision to prevent any damage.

The surgeon uses cold scissors to carefully perform ureterolysis, removing the periureteric endometriotic sheath. The dissection progresses cranially into a less disturbed plane, opening the tissue around the ureter.

The surgeon proceeds to excise the involved tissues, having separated the ureter from its periureteric endometriotic tissue sheath. The ureter is safeguarded on the left side, and the diseased tissue is being removed. The ureter appears supple, with the periureteric plane looking supple and its vascularity well preserved.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis at the crossing over of the ureter, excising the last part of the endometriotic tissue. The posterior planes have been developed, and the tissue is hitched up superiorly for complete release. A portion of the involved peritoneum is also excised to prevent recurrence.

The surgeon performs a right adnexectomy due to severe endometriosis, removing the right adnexa and excising the indurated endometriotic tissue. The retroperitoneal spaces are opened, revealing supple tissues.
And we will not go into the details of this clip as this is fairly simple.
The surgeon removes the right fallopian tube and ovary, followed by suture ligation of the right infundibulopelvic ligament.
The final view shows the surgical field after the excision of endometriotic tissue, with the ureter visible and safeguarded, and the periureteric plane appearing supple with preserved vascularity.
